edges.description: CHRNA4, CHRNA2, and CHRNB2 variants alter receptor function.
DEPDC5 loss-of-function disrupts GATOR1 control of mTOR signaling.
KCNT1 variants alter sodium-activated potassium-channel effects on cortical excitability.
Altered nicotinic signaling perturbs cortical excitatory and inhibitory synaptic dynamics.
mTOR-related focal epilepsy biology increases seizure-prone cortical network activity.
KCNT1 effects shift firing properties across cortical excitatory and inhibitory neurons.
Seizure-prone cortical networks manifest as brief stereotyped hypermotor nocturnal events.

edges.source: Vagus nerve stimulation for refractory ADSHE

evidence.snippet: CHRNA2 | cholinergic receptor nicotinic alpha 2 subunit | hgnc:1956 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
Here, we report the third mutation in the CHRNA2, found in a patient showing ADNFLE.
CHRNA4 | cholinergic receptor nicotinic alpha 4 subunit | hgnc:1958 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
The mutation is present in all 21 available affected family members and in four obligate carriers, but not in 333 healthy control subjects.
CHRNB2 | cholinergic receptor nicotinic beta 2 subunit | hgnc:1962 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

The newly identified locus associated with ENFL3 harbours several candidate genes, including CHRNB2 (ref. 8), whose gene product, the beta 2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) subunit, co-assembles with the alpha 4 nAChR subunit to form the active receptor.

nodes.description: Carbamazepine is the best-established first-line antiseizure medication for ADSHE and is associated with remission in many affected individuals.

evidence.snippet: Carbamazepine is associated with remission in about 70% of individuals, often in relatively low doses.

nodes.description: Convergent receptor, ion-channel, and mTOR mechanisms disturb the balance of excitatory and inhibitory cortical network activity, creating a seizure prone circuit state.

evidence.snippet: alters the local connectivity during synaptogenesis.
generally attributed to neocortical hyperexcitability in the frontal regions
DEPDC5 | DEP domain containing 5, GATOR1 subcomplex subunit | hgnc:18423 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) (loss of function) in
DEPDC5 loss-of-function mutations were found in 13% of the families with a presentation of ADNFLE.

nodes.description: DEPDC5 loss-of-function reduces GATOR1 complex integrity, releasing downstream mTORC1 signaling and increasing risk of focal epilepsy and focal cortical dysplasia-associated SHE.

evidence.snippet: In addition, 3 nonsense DEPDC5 mutations (p.Arg487*, p.Arg1087*, and p.Trp1369*) were detected in the probands of 2 French and one Belgian family.

Loss of Depdc5 led to a decrease in the other GATOR1 protein levels (NPRL2 and NPRL3). Rapamycin failed to rescue GATOR1 protein levels but rather rescued downstream mTORC1 hyperactivity as measured by phosphorylation of S6.

nodes.description: Selected genotypes may require alternatives or adjuncts, including zonisamide for some CHRNA4 variants, quinidine for KCNT1-related ADSHE, and fenofibrate as an adjunct for standard-ASM-resistant disease.

evidence.snippet: Individuals with ADSHE associated with the CHRNA4 pathogenic variant p.Ser284Leu are more responsive to zonisamide than carbamazepine. KCNT1-related ADSHE is difficult to treat but may be treatable using quinidine based on limited data.
Adjunctive fenofibrate therapy or vagal nerve stimulation may be considered in individuals resistant to standard ASM.

nodes.description: Familial SHE is initiated by heterozygous pathogenic variants in a genetically heterogeneous autosomal dominant set that includes nicotinic acetylcholine receptor genes, KCNT1, DEPDC5/GATOR1-related genes, and rarer reported loci.

evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of ADSHE is established in a proband who has suggestive clinical findings and a family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance and/or a heterozygous pathogenic variant in CABP4, CHRNA4, CHRNA2, CHRNB2, CRH, DEPDC5, KCNT1, NPRL2, NPRL3, or STX1B identified by molecular genetic testing.
KCNT1 | potassium sodium-activated channel subfamily T member 1 | hgnc:18865 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in

nodes.description: KCNT1 variants alter Slack sodium-activated potassium-channel behavior and can produce cortical network hyperexcitability through cell-type-specific effects on excitatory and inhibitory neurons.

evidence.snippet: Gain-of-function mutations in humans cause cortical network hyperexcitability, seizures, and severe intellectual disability.

nodes.description: CHRNA4, CHRNB2, and CHRNA2 pathogenic variants change alpha/beta nicotinic acetylcholine receptor function, altering cholinergic modulation of excitatory and inhibitory cortical transmission.

evidence.snippet: From this, we concluded that mutations that cause ADNFLE manifest themselves in a change in efficacy regardless of the stoichiometry of the receptor.
We conclude that mutations in CHRNA2 are more commonly linked to ADNFLE than previously thought, and may cause a loss-of-function phenotype.

nodes.description: Sleep-associated seizures are the core phenotype and typically occur as recurrent nocturnal clusters.

evidence.snippet: HP:0031951 | Nocturnal seizures | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: The final clinical expression is clusters of stereotyped, brief, sleep-associated motor seizures that can include hyperkinetic, tonic, dystonic, arousal-like, or wandering behaviors.

evidence.snippet: A rare seizure disorder characterized by intermittent dystonia and/or choreoathetoid movements that occur during sleep.

Autosomal dominant sleep-related hypermotor (hyperkinetic) epilepsy (ADSHE) is a seizure disorder characterized by clusters of nocturnal motor seizures that are often stereotyped and brief (<2 minutes).

nodes.description: Vagus nerve stimulation is a nonpharmacologic adjunctive option for individuals with ADSHE that remains resistant to standard antiseizure medications.
nodes.id: Vagus nerve stimulation for refractory ADSHE

evidence.snippet: Adjunctive fenofibrate therapy or vagal nerve stimulation may be considered in individuals resistant to standard ASM.
